openaireview__claude.label: OpenAIReview (claude)
openaireview__claude.overall_feedback: The core contribution — that Pam2Cys-adjuvanted Spike delivered intranasally produces stronger lung-localised IgA, neutralising antibody, and IL-17+ CD4+ T-cell responses than subcutaneous delivery, and confers protection against ancestral SARS-CoV-2 challenge in K18-hACE2 mice — is plausible and the dataset is rich, but several issues threaten the paper-level conclusions as currently framed. Most importantly: (1) the 'sterilising immunity' headline depends on a plaque-assay LOD that is never specified, day-7 lung sampling only, and n=5; (2) the BALB/c TLR2 chimera mechanism is never validated on the C57BL/6/K18-hACE2 strain used for protection, and no Tlr2-/- challenge experiment is performed; (3) Pam2Cys-only i.n. confers 60% no detectable virus and 40% no symptoms, which is statistically indistinguishable from the Spike+Pam2Cys arm at this n and materially confounds attribution of protection to Spike-specific immunity; (4) the URT and transmission readouts that motivate mucosal vaccination are absent. These are fixable with additional experiments and a more conservative reframing — the work is worth revising rather than rejecting — but the present claims of 'sterilising', 'complete', and TLR2-mechanism-driven protection should be substantially softened or supported by the missing data.

comments.title: "Sterilising immunity" claim is LOD-dependent, day-7-only, lung-only — overstates what was measured
comments.quote: Consistent with the substantial antibody response generated by Pam_{2}Cys Spike, vaccination by either route induced sterilizing protection with no virus detected in the lung tissue at day 7 post infection (Fig. 6f).
comments.explanation: The headline 'sterilising' claim — used in the Results section title, the Discussion ('complete protection against viral infection in the lung tissue'), and implicit in the abstract framing — rests on a single negative readout: absence of detectable virus by plaque assay in lung homogenate at day 7 post-infection in n=5 mice per group. Multiple problems compound: (1) The plaque assay limit of detection is never stated or shown on the figure, so 'no virus detected' is consistent with sub-LOD virus rather than zero. (2) Day 7 is past the natural peak of viral replication in K18-hACE2 (typically day 2-4) — viral clearance is already occurring in many animals at this timepoint regardless of vaccination, so a 0/5 negative result is uninformative about whether replication was prevented vs delayed. (3) The plaque assay sample is a homogenate of one lung lobe; volume of homogenate is not stated, so PFU values are not normalised to tissue input mass. (4) Upper respiratory tract (nasal turbinates / trachea) — repeatedly highlighted in the Discussion as the key site for transmission and the differentiator of mucosal vaccines — was not sampled. With these caveats, the appropriate claim is 'no detectable virus in lung at day 7', not 'sterilising immunity'.

comments.title: Strain mismatch across experimental modules: chimera-derived TLR2 mechanism is never validated on the strain used for the protective challenge
comments.quote: irradiated wild type (WT; BALB/c) and TLR2 deficient (Tlr2-/-) mice were reconstituted with either WT or Tlr2-/- (KO) bone marrow
comments.explanation: The mechanistic chain the paper builds is: (a) i.n. Pam2Cys-Spike induces lung IgA and IL-17+ CD4 T cells (C57BL/6, Figs 1-2); (b) TLR2 on immune cells is 'required' for those responses (BALB/c chimeras, Figs 3-5); (c) i.n. Pam2Cys-Spike protects against challenge (K18-hACE2 on C57BL/6 background, Figs 6-7). Step (b) is the only experiment that interrogates TLR2-dependence and is performed on a different genetic background (BALB/c) than steps (a) and (c). C57BL/6 and BALB/c differ markedly in their canonical Th1/Th2 polarisation, in MHC haplotype (H2-b vs H2-d) and consequently in the Spike epitopes their CD4 T cells will recognise, in TLR-driven inflammation profiles, and in IL-17 vs IFN-γ skewing — the very axis the chimera findings are interpreted along. No experiment in the paper closes the loop on a single strain: there is no Tlr2-/- × K18-hACE2 (or Tlr2-/- C57BL/6) challenge, no BALB/c chimera challenge, and no rationale for the strain choice in the chimera arm. The Discussion nevertheless invokes the chimera-derived 'TLR2 on hematopoietic cells is essential' conclusion to explain the C57BL/6/K18-hACE2 protection. Without a same-strain test of TLR2-dependence, the claim that TLR2 stimulation drives the protective response is not directly supported.

comments.title: Radiation-resistant alveolar macrophages confound the 'TLR2 on hematopoietic cells' conclusion
comments.quote: Mice were rested for 12-weeks to allow hematopoietic reconstitution and replacement of radiation-resistant populations in the lungs and airways.
comments.explanation: Alveolar macrophages (SiglecF+CD11c-hi) — explicitly one of the populations the authors quantify in Fig 3d, and at the adaptive timepoint (Fig 4 panels, Fig S7) one of the clusters with the highest TLR2 expression — are an embryonically seeded, locally self-renewing tissue-resident population that is well established to be largely radiation-resistant. After 800 RAD irradiation followed by 12 weeks of rest, the donor-derived contribution to the AM pool is well below 50% in most published time-courses, and is highly variable between animals. The paper acknowledges 'radiation-resistant populations' obliquely in the rest-period justification but does not (a) report per-animal AM chimerism, (b) show that AMs are CD45-congenic-distinguishable in their Spectre clustering, or (c) gate AM-specific TLR2 expression by donor origin. The downstream interpretive framework — that adaptive responses depend on TLR2 expression by 'immune cells' meaning hematopoietic-derived APCs — is therefore confounded: a substantial fraction of the TLR2+ APCs in the WT-recipient/KO-donor (WT/KO BM) chimera lungs are still host-origin radiation-resistant AMs (which are TLR2+), while in the KO-recipient/WT-donor (KO/WT BM) animals these same AMs are TLR2-deficient. The 'epithelial vs hematopoietic' dichotomy as designed cannot cleanly isolate TLR2 contributions.

comments.title: Pam2Cys-only i.n. arm shows substantial nonspecific protection that materially confounds the Spike-specific narrative
comments.quote: Intriguingly, 60% of mice receiving Pam_{2}Cys i.n also had no detectable virus in lung tissue.
comments.explanation: The paper's central thesis is that a Spike-antigen-specific adaptive response (IgG, IgA, neutralising antibodies, IL-17+ lung-resident CD4 T cells) drives protection against SARS-CoV-2 challenge. Yet in Fig 6, animals receiving Pam2Cys i.n. with NO Spike antigen — i.e., that have neither anti-Spike antibody nor Spike-specific T cells — achieve 40% no clinical disease and 60% no detectable lung virus. With n=5 per group, 60% (3/5) versus 100% (5/5) in the Pam2Cys-Spike i.n. arm is not statistically distinguishable (Fisher exact p≈0.44). This means the data cannot statistically separate adjuvant-only nonspecific protection from Spike-specific protection in the i.n. route — the very route that drives the paper's headline result. The Discussion frames the partial protection as a 'beneficial nonspecific innate priming effect' (citing TLR2-agonist studies in rhinovirus/influenza), but does not acknowledge that this same mechanism plausibly accounts for a large share of the protection attributed in this paper to the Spike antigen. A larger n, additional comparator arms (e.g., Spike-only i.n., adjuvant-only s.c. with parallel readouts), and per-mouse correlation between immunogenicity readouts and protection, would be required to attribute protection to Spike-specific immunity rather than innate priming.

comments.title: Upper respiratory tract viral load and transmission — the explicit motivation of the paper — were never measured
comments.quote: Studies in non-human primates demonstrate a correlation between increasing serum nAb titres and protection in the URT, so that lower nAb titres are sufficient to protect the lung alone, compared to higher nAb levels needed to protect both the URT and lungs.
comments.explanation: The Introduction and Discussion repeatedly motivate mucosal vaccination by the failure of parenteral platforms to control URT replication and onward transmission. Direct quotes from the Introduction: 'mucosal vaccination... may provide protection against infection and minimise viral spread'; 'a major need to continue investing in vaccine development for SARS-CoV-2'; 'The generation of mucosal immunity, in the URT where viral replication first occurs...' The Discussion goes further: 'there is greater variability in protection against URT infection and transmission' for current parenteral vaccines. Yet the only viral-load readout in the challenge experiment is lung tissue plaque assay (Fig 6f). Nasal turbinate, trachea, oral/nasal swab measurements — technically standard in K18-hACE2 SARS-CoV-2 work and feasible at the same necropsy timepoint — are absent. No transmission-to-naïve-cagemate experiment is performed (the Discussion lists this as future work). The strongest claim the dataset can support is therefore 'protects against lower-respiratory disease', which is the same domain that intramuscular vaccines already cover. The paper's distinguishing claim — superiority for URT/transmission — is asserted but not tested.

comments.title: Cross-VOC efficacy claim inflated: in vitro neutralisation only, different cell system from headline assay, ancestral-Spike-only ELISA
comments.quote: Substantial nAb titres were generated against the ancestral strain, and while these titres were reduced against Beta and Delta strains, mucosal immunization induced significant and superior responses, with a mean nAb EC_{50} greater than 10^{3} against both VOCs (Fig. 6b).
comments.explanation: Three problems compound. (1) The cross-VOC claim — that the vaccine retains efficacy against Beta and Delta — is supported only by in vitro neutralisation EC50 on HEK-ACE2-TMPRSS2 cells. The protective challenge in vivo (Fig 6c-f, Fig 7) uses ancestral A.2.2 SARS-CoV-2 only. In vitro neutralisation is a correlate, not a substitute, for in vivo protection, particularly across VOCs where T-cell, IgA, and innate components matter. (2) Cell-system inconsistency: Fig 1 reports neutralisation in HEK-ACE2 (no TMPRSS2) and in VeroE6; Fig 6b uses HEK-ACE2-TMPRSS2. EC50 values from these three systems are not directly comparable in scale (TMPRSS2-mediated entry favours fusogenic Spikes; VeroE6 supports endosomal entry less efficiently than HEK-ACE2-TMPRSS2). The fold-reduction from ancestral to VOC reported in Fig 6b is therefore specific to the HEK-ACE2-TMPRSS2 readout and does not translate cleanly to the immunogenicity figures' reported potencies. (3) The ELISA antigen and the immunogen are both ancestral-strain prefusion HexaPro. Antibodies measured in Fig 1b/Fig 6a have not been shown to bind Beta or Delta Spike, only the homologous antigen. The rhetorical claim 'high titres against VOCs' conflates binding ELISA (ancestral) with neutralisation (VOC) without distinguishing between them, and the Discussion overstates what the data can support.

comments.title: Sample sizes are too small for the non-parametric tests used, and pooling across two biological experiments creates pseudo-replication
comments.quote: Data are for individual mice from two independent biological experiments (n=2--3 each), means +/- SEM are shown.
comments.explanation: Two compounding statistical problems. (1) Power: across the immunogenicity (Figs 1-2), chimera (Figs 3-5), and challenge (Figs 6-7) experiments, group sizes are reported as n=2-3 per experiment (totalling 4-6), n=2-6 per chimera arm (the Fig 5 legend explicitly admits n=2-6 even though the prose states n=6), and n=5 for challenge. With Mann-Whitney at n1=n2=3, the smallest achievable two-tailed p is 0.10 — i.e., it is mathematically impossible to reach the conventional α=0.05 in some pairwise tests the paper reports. Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn's post-hoc on n=4-6 per group has comparably low power, particularly across 4-arm chimera comparisons. No power calculation is reported anywhere in the Methods, and the variable n across panels (n=2-6 vs n=4-6) creates unbalanced post-hoc comparisons whose properties are not discussed. (2) Pseudo-replication: 'individual mice from two independent biological experiments' is treated as the analysis unit, but animals are nested within distinct experiments that differ in vaccine batch, BALF processing day, ELISA plate, pseudovirus prep, and operator. Without an experiment-level random effect (or, equivalently, presenting both experiments separately and showing concordance), the effective degrees of freedom are inflated, standard errors are underestimated, and apparent significance is overstated. The combination of small per-experiment n + pseudo-replication + non-parametric testing makes most reported p-values difficult to interpret.

comments.title: Reporting gap: no randomisation, blinding, exclusion criteria, sample-size justification, or pre-registered analysis plan (ARRIVE 2.0 noncompliance)
comments.quote: All animal experiments were conducted in full compliance with local and institutional guidelines, with approvals from the Sydney Local Health District Animal Welfare Committee.
comments.explanation: ARRIVE 2.0 — the de facto standard for animal-research reporting and a requirement of most journals in this space — calls for explicit reporting of: (a) randomisation method for group allocation, (b) blinding of operators scoring subjective endpoints (clinical score, histology, plaque counts), (c) any animal exclusions and pre-specified criteria for them, (d) the basis of group-size choice (power calculation or pilot data), and (e) a pre-specified primary endpoint and statistical analysis plan. None of these are reported in the Methods. This is particularly consequential here because (i) the clinical score (Fig 6d) is a subjective four-category readout that is highly susceptible to expectancy bias if read unblinded; (ii) the lung histology in Fig 7 reduces to inflammatory cell counts at 'a single 40x field' with no blinded scoring rubric described; (iii) post-hoc test selection (Welch's t vs Mann-Whitney; Dunn's vs Tukey's) is unconstrained by any pre-specified rule, leaving room for p-hacking across many panels. Combined with the absence of any data/code availability statement in Methods, the work is not currently reproducible or independently re-analysable.

comments.title: Anomalous chimera B-cell increase contradicts the dose-response logic the paper uses elsewhere
comments.quote: A significant increase of B-cells (B220^{+}CD11c^{-}) was apparent in Tlr2-/- mice receiving WT BM, but not in WT mice receiving WT BM.
comments.explanation: Within the paper's own framework, the WT-recipient/WT-donor (WT/WT) and Tlr2-/--recipient/WT-donor (KO/WT BM) groups differ only in TLR2 status of the non-hematopoietic compartment: WT/WT has TLR2 on both compartments, KO/WT has TLR2 only on the hematopoietic side. The KO/WT animals have strictly fewer TLR2-expressing compartments than WT/WT. Yet in this single anomaly, KO/WT shows MORE lung B cells than WT/WT — the opposite of what the additive 'TLR2-dose-response' framework used elsewhere in the paper would predict. The paper does not address this contradiction. Possible explanations include: (i) compensatory upregulation in TLR2-deficient hosts, (ii) radiation/BM-transfer artefact (e.g., differential reconstitution kinetics in Tlr2-/- recipients), (iii) noise from low n. None of these are discussed, but each would substantially weaken the broader claim that TLR2 dose-on-compartments translates monotonically to immune outcomes.

comments.title: Claim that 'epithelial TLR2 plays a key role' in early APC recruitment is overdrawn from the WT-recipient/KO-donor arm alone
comments.quote: Thus, activation of TLR2 on the respiratory epithelia plays a key role in recruiting these innate immune cells to the lungs early after mucosal vaccination.
comments.explanation: The conclusion that respiratory epithelial TLR2 drives DC, alveolar macrophage and monocyte recruitment rests on the WT-recipient/KO-donor (WT/KO BM) arm at 24 h post a single i.n. dose: this arm shows elevated APC counts vs the KO/KO control. But (1) this arm still contains host-origin radiation-resistant AMs that are themselves TLR2+ tissue-resident immune cells (see major issue on AM radioresistance), so the 'epithelial vs hematopoietic' dichotomy is not cleanly tested by this comparison. (2) 'Respiratory epithelia' is one of several non-hematopoietic compartments that retain TLR2 in WT/KO chimeras — endothelium, fibroblasts, smooth muscle cells, and resident lung macrophages also express TLR2. The design cannot specifically attribute the recruitment effect to epithelium versus other non-hematopoietic compartments. (3) The Fig 3d effect is at 24 h after a single i.n. dose, but the immunogenicity outcomes the paper most cares about are at 1 wk after three doses; the early innate readout is not shown to causally drive the late adaptive readout (no kinetic chase or perturbation). The phrasing 'plays a key role' should be softened to 'is associated with' unless an epithelial-specific TLR2 conditional KO is used.

comments.title: ELISA antigen identical to immunogen and lenient cutoff (mean+3SD) inflate apparent antibody titres
comments.quote: High-binding ELISA plates (Corning Falcon) were coated overnight at 4°C with Spike protein (1 μg/ml) in carbonate/bicarbonate coating buffer (0.05 M, pH 9.6).
comments.explanation: Two assay-design issues compound. (1) The Methods do not specify which Spike preparation coats the ELISA plate, but the only recombinant Spike described is the prefusion-stabilised HexaPro construct used for vaccination. If the same HexaPro is the ELISA capture antigen, the assay measures binding to the very conformation the immune system was exposed to, including epitopes specific to the engineered prolines. This systematically inflates titres relative to assays that use native trimer or VOC variants, and over-reports cross-reactivity to wild-type Spike. The ELISA cannot, on its own, support a binding claim against Beta or Delta Spike. (2) The titre cutoff is operationally defined as the dilution interpolating mean+3SD of the unvaccinated 1:100 sera (or 1:40 BALF) — there is no calibration against a reference standard, no assay-validation panel, and no characterisation of inter-plate or inter-day CV. With small unvaccinated reference sample sizes (n=2-3), 'mean+3SD' is itself a noisy estimate. The combination overstates discriminability and biases comparisons across plates and days.

comments.title: Spike protein endotoxin and aggregate quality not reported despite TLR-driven readouts
comments.quote: Doses per vaccination were as follows: 7.7 μg Pam_{2}Cys-SK_{4}-triethylene glycolate and 6 μg Spike, admixed in sterile cell culture grade phosphate buffered saline, endotoxin tested (PBS; Sigma D8537).
comments.explanation: Only the PBS is described as endotoxin-tested; the recombinant Spike protein purified from Expi293F cells is not. A TLR4-active LPS contaminant of even low ng/mg amounts in the Spike preparation would directly confound a study whose central claim concerns TLR2-specific adjuvancy: the Tlr2-/- chimera readouts would still receive innate signaling from contaminating LPS via the WT donor compartment (TLR4 is intact). Likewise, gel-filtration step is described, but no analytical SEC trace, percent monomer/aggregate, dynamic light scattering data, or mass-spec confirmation of the prefusion conformation are reported. Aggregated Spike has a different, more potent innate-stimulating profile than monomeric trimer, and could mimic adjuvant-like effects without TLR2 engagement. For a paper that hinges on TLR2-specificity, basic protein-quality and endotoxin reporting are essential.

comments.title: BM chimera reconstitution efficiency is not verified per animal before vaccination
comments.quote: Mice were rested for 12 weeks to allow hematopoietic reconstitution.
comments.explanation: The chimera design's central inference depends on cleanly dichotomising TLR2 status between hematopoietic and non-hematopoietic compartments. The Methods state a 12-week rest after 800 RAD irradiation and 1.8×10^6 BM cells transfer, but do NOT describe (a) verification of donor chimerism in any tissue (e.g., by CD45.1/CD45.2 flow on peripheral blood pre-vaccination, or by genotyping the Tlr2 locus in blood leukocytes), (b) the percentage donor chimerism considered acceptable, or (c) any animal exclusions for incomplete reconstitution. The Results §2 verification — 'TLR2 expression by FIt-SNE was examined for each experimental group' — is qualitative, group-level, and downstream of the experimental endpoint. Per-animal confirmation that hematopoietic TLR2 status corresponded to the intended chimera condition would substantially strengthen any compartment-attribution claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent statistical test selection within the same experimental design (Welch's t for Fig 1b, Kruskal-Wallis for Fig 1c-d)
comments.quote: Statistics: p-values indicated, (b) Welch's t test, (c, d) Kruskal-Wallis test, Dunn's multiple comparisons.
comments.explanation: Figure 1 has three groups (UNV, s.c., i.n.) with the same n=4-6 per group across panels. Welch's t is a two-group test, so its application to panel (b) implies pairwise testing without an omnibus step (and therefore no correction across the three pairwise contrasts), while panels (c, d) use omnibus Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn's correction. The two regimes differ markedly in their type-I error control: Welch's t with no correction is the most permissive, Kruskal-Wallis/Dunn's the most conservative. There is no rationale for switching between them on data from the same experimental design. This pattern of choice — parametric/no-correction where it is favourable and non-parametric/with-correction where it is not — recurs across panels (e.g., Mann-Whitney for Fig 6a, Kruskal-Wallis for Fig 6e-f). Without a pre-specified rule for test choice (e.g., based on Shapiro-Wilk normality), the analysis is selection-biased toward 'significant' calls.

comments.title: Female-only design across three strains is never disclosed as a generalisability limitation
comments.quote: Female C57BL/6 (6--8 week-old) or BALB/c (various ages) mice were sourced from Animal BioResources... Female Tlr2-/- mice (BALB/c background) were bred at the Centenary Institute. Female K18-hACE2 mice were sourced from the Jackson Laboratory.
comments.explanation: All cohorts (immunogenicity, chimera, and challenge) are female-only. There is no rationale provided in the Methods, no mention of sex in the Discussion, and no statement of generalisability constraints. Sex-as-a-biological-variable is an explicit NIH/ARRIVE expectation. Mucosal IgA induction, TLR2-driven inflammation, and SARS-CoV-2 disease severity in K18-hACE2 mice are all known to differ between sexes, so a female-only design constrains the conclusions to female mice. The Introduction promises broader population coverage ('young, aged, immunocompromised'), making the silence on sex more conspicuous.

comments.title: Pseudovirus assay lacks input normalisation and a positive neutralisation control
comments.quote: Pseudovirus particles were incubated with serially diluted serum or BALF samples at 37°C, 5% CO_{2} for 1 hr prior to spinoculation (800 xg, 35°C) of ACE2 over-expressing HEK293T cells.
comments.explanation: Several reproducibility-critical parameters are missing from the pseudovirus neutralisation assay used to support the headline nAb titres in Fig 1c. (1) Input pseudovirus titre (e.g., target percentage GFP-positive cells in the no-serum control, TCID50, p24 normalisation, or RLU range) is not specified. EC50 shifts substantially with input dose, so without an input target the absolute titres are not comparable across experiments or to the literature. (2) No positive control (e.g., a reference convalescent serum, a published mAb such as REGN10987 or LY-CoV1404) is used to anchor the assay's potency scale. (3) The ACE2 over-expressing HEK293T line is identified by ATCC catalogue but transduction conditions, ACE2 expression level, and clonal selection are described as 'previously described' rather than re-stated. Without these, reported pseudovirus EC50 values cannot be cross-referenced or independently validated.

comments.title: Innate (24 h, single dose) and adaptive (1 wk after three doses) chimera readouts cannot be linked to each other in the same animals
comments.quote: To assess early innate responses to mucosal vaccination, mice were immunized i.n with Pam_{2}Cys Spike once, then immune populations of the lungs and airways examined by flow cytometry at 24-hours post vaccination (Fig. 3a).
comments.explanation: The paper's mechanistic narrative is that early innate recruitment (Fig 3, 24 h after a single dose) drives the later adaptive responses (Figs 4-5, 1 wk after three doses) and ultimately the protection (Fig 6, 3 wk after three doses). But these are separate cohorts of animals with different doses, schedules, and timepoints. There is no kinetic measurement, no within-animal pairing, and no perturbation that selectively blocks early innate recruitment to test whether it causally produces the later adaptive readouts. The early innate panel is therefore correlative and descriptive — it cannot, on its own, support a causal role for the early TLR2-mediated APC recruitment in driving the protective immunogenicity. The Discussion uses 'leads to' language ('TLR2 stimulation of the pulmonary epithelia... recruit neutrophils and activate APCs') that overstates what is shown.

comments.title: Absent CD8+ Spike-specific response leaves the protective mechanism mechanistically incomplete
comments.quote: No significantly increased populations of Spike-specific CD8^{+} T-cells secreting IFNγ, IL-2 or TNF were detected (Fig. 2c,d).
comments.explanation: This is a non-trivial negative finding for a candidate SARS-CoV-2 vaccine. CD8+ T cells are widely considered important for clearing infected airway epithelial cells and for cross-variant protection in scenarios where antibody escape occurs (the same VOC scenario the paper invokes elsewhere). The absence of a Spike-specific CD8+ signal in either lung or spleen — at any of the cytokines screened — has direct implications for the durability and breadth of protection that the abstract and Discussion claim, particularly in the context of 'cross-protection against other pathogenic coronaviruses' the Discussion proposes. The paper reports this finding in a single sentence and does not engage with what it implies (e.g., is the protection antibody-dominant? CD4-only? would the vaccine fail when nAb wanes?). A candidate pulmonary vaccine for a virus where CD8 immunity is increasingly seen as important should at least discuss whether this absence is a concern, an opportunity for additional antigens (NP) as the Discussion mentions in passing, or an artefact of the recall-stimulation conditions.

comments.title: Discussion's 'future work' list substitutes for honest present-tense limitations
comments.quote: Future studies will require an assessment of the durability of protective responses achievable with this vaccination approach, including in the URT such as the nasal mucosa and trachea, for which SARS-CoV-2 demonstrates a notable tropism.
comments.explanation: The Discussion enumerates several substantive issues — durability, URT immunity, transmission, prime-pull strategies, hamster/ferret models — exclusively as future work, framed as opportunities rather than as limitations of the present study. This framing matters: stated as future work, these become extensions; stated as limitations, they constrain the current paper's claims. The strongest example is URT immunity: the present manuscript repeatedly motivates mucosal vaccination by URT/transmission control yet measures only lung viral load (see major issue), and the Discussion acknowledges this only by saying future work 'will require an assessment' of URT. A candid limitations paragraph that lists what the data cannot support — 'we did not measure URT viral load and therefore cannot claim transmission-blocking efficacy', 'durability beyond 3 weeks post final dose was not tested', 'protection against Beta or Delta in vivo was not tested', 'the chimera mechanism was not tested in the strain used for protection' — would substantially strengthen the paper's calibration.

comments.title: Lung IL-17+ CD4+ T-cell skew is presented neutrally without engaging vaccine-enhanced respiratory disease (VERD) literature
comments.quote: Mucosally immunized mice had a substantial population of Spike-specific CD4^{+} T-cells locally in the lungs, predominantly secreting IL-17 (Fig. 2a)
comments.explanation: An IL-17-dominant lung-resident CD4+ T-cell response is presented as a positive readout. However, prior literature on respiratory viral vaccines — most notably the FI-RSV (formalin-inactivated RSV) catastrophic vaccine trial of the 1960s and several early SARS-CoV vaccine candidates — has flagged Th17/Th2-skewed lung infiltrates as associated with vaccine-enhanced respiratory disease (VERD), characterised by neutrophilic immunopathology on subsequent live virus challenge. The paper does not engage with this concern, despite the present challenge data showing substantial neutrophil influx in BALF (Fig 6e) and inflammatory cell counts in lung histology (Fig 7) even in protected groups. The Discussion's own observation that 'IL-17+ CD4+ T-cell response... is typical following mucosal delivery of protein-based vaccines' should be paired with an explicit statement that VERD risk was monitored for and not observed (with histological criteria), and a discussion of why the IL-17 skew in this model is benign.

comments.title: Introduction's TLR2/Pam2Cys rationale is heavily self-cited and based on bacterial (Mtb) precedent rather than viral disease
comments.quote: We and others have recently shown that the TLR2 ligand, Pam2Cys, is a highly effective mucosal adjuvant for peptide or protein-based vaccines. Pulmonary immunization with mycobacterial peptides or protein conjugated to Pam2Cys stimulated protective immunity in the lung against Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection.
comments.explanation: The motivation for choosing TLR2 (vs other PRRs that drive mucosal immunity — e.g., TLR3, TLR7/8, STING agonists, cGAMP) is given as a pair of self-citations to the authors' Mtb work (refs 26-28) plus refs 33-35 on TLR2 prophylactic protection in respiratory virus models. The Mtb precedent is for a bacterial pathogen with very different vaccine-immunology requirements than a respiratory virus. The Introduction does not engage with the substantial literature comparing PRR-targeted mucosal adjuvants in viral disease, nor explain why TLR2 was chosen over alternatives that have entered SARS-CoV-2 mucosal vaccine development. The phrasing 'we and others' overstates what the cited evidence base shows for viral-disease applications specifically; the chimera experiments in this paper are the primary new evidence for TLR2's compartmental role in viral antigen response, but the Introduction frames TLR2 as already-established for the use case.

comments.title: 'Novel subunit vaccine' framing despite multiple prior Pam2Cys mucosal vaccines from the same and adjacent groups
comments.quote: Thus, we examined the pattern of lung and systemic immune responses induced by a novel subunit vaccine, consisting of recombinant trimeric SARS-CoV-2 Spike protein admixed with the TLR2-stimulating adjuvant Pam2Cys (Pam2Cys Spike), delivered via the mucosal or systemic route.
comments.explanation: The vaccine is described as 'novel'. While the Spike+Pam2Cys-SK4-triethylene glycolate combination for SARS-CoV-2 may be new, Pam2Cys-adjuvanted mucosal protein/peptide vaccines for other pathogens are referenced multiple times in the same paper (refs 26-30, 33-35), several of which include the same authors. In context, 'novel' overstates the conceptual contribution; 'the first application of the Pam2Cys-SK4 lipopeptide adjuvant to recombinant SARS-CoV-2 Spike' would more accurately scope the claim of novelty. This is a minor framing concern but it appears in the abstract-level positioning of the work.

comments.title: K18-hACE2 model limitations not acknowledged when introducing the challenge model
comments.quote: Finally, the protective efficacy of the vaccine following mucosal and parenteral delivery was assessed in the K18-hACE2 murine model of SARS-CoV-2 infection.
comments.explanation: The K18-hACE2 model has well-documented limitations relevant to the paper's claims: (1) hACE2 expression under the K18 (cytokeratin-18) promoter drives expression in lung epithelium and brain, leading to substantial CNS pathology that can dominate clinical scoring and mortality independently of pulmonary protection; (2) the model is comparatively poor at recapitulating human URT pathology and transmission — the very domains the paper most wants to address; (3) lethal-challenge data with this model are influenced by inoculation dose and route in ways not directly translatable to human infection. The Introduction names the model and cites a methods reference (ref 31, ref 57) but does not mention these caveats. The Discussion notes that 'animal models, such as hamsters or ferrets, could be used' as future work but does not flag the K18-hACE2 limitations as a present constraint.

comments.title: BALF readouts are reported without normalisation to lavage recovery volume
comments.quote: BALF was obtained by tracheal intubation, inflation of the airways with 1 ml PBS and collection of the fluid.
comments.explanation: BALF antibody titres (Fig 1b, Fig 6a), neutralisation EC50 (Fig 1c-d, Fig 5b), and total leukocyte counts (Fig 6e) are reported as concentrations or counts. Lavage recovery volume varies substantially mouse-to-mouse (typically 60-90% of input depending on technique and lung pathology), which directly affects the analyte concentration in the recovered fluid. Normalisation to recovered volume, total protein, urea (a standard dilution marker), or input volume is not described. This is particularly relevant for the challenge experiment where infected mice may have altered alveolar fluid retention compared with naïve animals, potentially biasing across-group BALF comparisons.

comments.title: Lung histology stain (modified Giemsa) is non-standard for SARS-CoV-2 challenge readout
comments.quote: Histology sections were stained using Quick Dip Stain Kit (Modified Giemsa Stain) protocol as per manufacturer's instructions.
comments.explanation: Lung histopathology in SARS-CoV-2 challenge models is conventionally read by H&E with a published, blinded, semi-quantitative score capturing perivascular cuffing, alveolar wall thickening, intra-alveolar exudate, hyaline membranes, and so on. Here, modified Giemsa (a haematological stain not optimised for tissue architecture) is used, and the readout is reduced to 'inflammatory cell counts' at a single 40× field with no statement of how many fields were scored, who scored them, or whether scorers were blinded. This is a presentation/methodology choice with a downstream cost: the histology data in Fig 7 are difficult to compare to the broader K18-hACE2 challenge literature because they are not on the same scoring system.
